planting.note: Direct sow at last frost in dry, lean, well-drained sandy or rocky soil in full sun; self-sows reliably in open disturbed ground.

directions: Annual Wild Buckwheat is a strongly upright native annual of dry, open prairies, roadsides, and rocky or sandy plains throughout the Great Plains and south-central US. It produces dense, woolly-white, branching flower clusters of tiny white to cream-white flowers from July through October on stout stems with white-hairy, oblong basal leaves. The dense flower heads and seeds are outstanding food sources for goldfinches, sparrows, and native bees. Direct sow in spring in dry, lean, well-drained sandy or rocky soil in full sun — this species self-sows reliably and thrives in disturbed ground with minimal care.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow cold stratification over winter; cold exposure improves germination rates significantly.

directions: Longleaf Wild Buckwheat is a native perennial of dry, rocky or sandy open ground throughout the southeastern and south-central US — one of the few Eriogonum species native east of Texas. It produces mounded clumps of long, narrow, woolly-white basal leaves and slender upright flowering stems bearing clusters of small white to pale cream flowers in summer, followed by seed heads heavily consumed by native sparrows and finches. Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Established plants are notably drought-tolerant and long-lived once rooted into well-drained ground.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Start seed indoors with a cold stratification period of 60-90 days before transplanting in spring; plant into very gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Shale Barren Wild Buckwheat is a rare endemic restricted to the ancient shale barrens of West Virginia and Virginia — one of only a handful of Eriogonum species native to eastern North America. It forms a compact, rosette-shaped perennial with woolly, spoon-shaped leaves and open clusters of yellow to cream-yellow flowers in summer, thriving on extreme, thin, hot, and dry rocky shale soils where little else survives. Grow in very well-drained, gritty, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun, and avoid any organic-rich or moisture-retentive amendments that encourage rot. This species is an exceptional choice for specialized rock and alpine garden collections in zones 4-7.
sources: Center for Plant Conservation — Shale Barren Endemics

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into deep, loose, sandy soil; the papery seed bracts can be left intact or removed to improve germination speed.

directions: Fragrant Sand Verbena is a sprawling, glandular-sticky native perennial of dry, sandy prairies, dunes, and roadsides of the Great Plains and Rocky Mountain foothills. It produces dense, ball-like clusters of small, intensely fragrant white to pale pinkish tubular flowers in spring and summer that open in the evening and release a powerful sweet vanilla-clove fragrance highly attractive to hawk moths at dusk. Direct sow in fall in deep, loose, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun, or start indoors in deep containers to accommodate the taproot — this plant is not suited to heavy, clay, or waterlogged soils. An outstanding fragrant native for moonlight and pollinator gardens across zones 4-8.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall on bare sandy soil in zones 8-11; soak papery seed coats overnight or nick lightly before sowing to improve germination.

directions: Desert Sand Verbena is a stunning desert annual native to sandy flats, bajadas, and washes of the Mojave and Sonoran deserts of California, Arizona, and Baja California — one of the most spectacular early-spring wildflowers of California superbloom events. Its sticky, glandular-hairy, sprawling stems are covered in large dome-shaped clusters of vivid magenta-pink to rose-purple, sweetly fragrant tubular flowers from February through April. Direct sow in fall on bare, sandy, well-drained soil in zones 8-11; soak the papery seed coats overnight or nick them lightly before sowing to improve germination rates. Desert Sand Verbena does not transplant well and should always be direct sown where it is to grow.
